_meta.title: How LLMs Learn — What actually happens inside the box
sources: Schaeffer, Miranda, Koyejo 2023 (arXiv:2304.15004, NeurIPS Best Paper)
_meta.note: Real numbers from public papers, model cards, and Epoch AI's notable-models database. Where a number is estimated (e.g., GPT-4 architecture rumors), it is labelled.

scaling._meta: Loss vs compute on a log-log axis. Each model is plotted at its training compute (FLOPs) and approximate pretraining loss. Chinchilla optimum line follows D = 20 × N (Hoffmann 2022).

models.name: GPT-4 (est.)
models.note: MoE; ~280B active. Numbers are press estimates.

annotations.label: Llama 3 overshoots tokens 10× — for inference, not training-optimal

training_stack._meta: Three (or four) post-2022 stages, with typical compute share, dataset size, and what each stage teaches. Numbers from Llama 3 paper (arXiv:2407.21783), DeepSeek-R1 paper, OpenAI/Anthropic technical reports.

stages.label: Pretraining
stages.data: Common Crawl + FineWeb + books + code + Wikipedia
stages.what_it_teaches: World knowledge, language patterns, the underlying probability distribution over text
stages.duration: Weeks to months on thousands of GPUs

stages.label: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT)
stages.examples: Llama 3 reports ~10M SFT examples; OpenAI InstructGPT used ~13k human demos
stages.what_it_teaches: How to follow instructions and respond in conversational format

stages.label: RLHF / DPO
stages.examples: InstructGPT: 33k comparisons · Anthropic HH-RLHF: 161k · Llama 3 DPO: ~1M
stages.what_it_teaches: Helpfulness, harmlessness, tone — the 'voice' of an assistant

stages.label: Reasoning-RL (o1 / R1)
stages.objective: Reward verifiable correct chains of reasoning
stages.data: Math, code, logic problems with checkable answers
stages.tokens: Hundreds of thousands of long-CoT trajectories
stages.examples: DeepSeek-R1: pure RL after small cold-start SFT · OpenAI o1 family
stages.what_it_teaches: How to think before answering — chain-of-thought as a learned policy
stages.duration: Days, but inference compute scales separately

tokenization._meta: Vocabulary sizes have grown ~5× since GPT-2 to better cover code, multiple languages, and rare characters. Tokenization is BPE (byte-pair encoding) for almost every modern model; specifics differ.
why_it_matters: Math: GPT-2 splits numbers into 3-digit chunks, hurting multi-digit arithmetic. GPT-4 keeps longer chunks together.
Code: doubling vocabulary roughly halves token count for source code, lowering inference cost
Multilingual: small vocabs disadvantage non-English text, where 1 character can become 4–6 tokens
Karpathy's bug list: trailing whitespace, mixed-case, Unicode normalization can all change tokenization invisibly

transformer._meta: Architecture parameter breakdown for a modern decoder-only Transformer (Llama 3 70B as the canonical example).

components.label: Attention (Q,K,V,O)
components.note: With GQA, K and V are smaller

components.label: MLP (gate, up, down)
components.note: Where most params live in modern LLMs

transformer.attention_math: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k) · V — Vaswani et al. 2017. Q queries the K keys to produce a weighted average over V values.

dynamics._meta: What 'training' actually looks like across the public training runs that have published checkpoints (OLMo 2, Pythia, BLOOM).

phenomena.label: Smooth log-linear decay
phenomena.summary: Pretraining loss is shockingly smooth. Plot training loss against log(steps) and you get a near-straight line for trillions of tokens.
phenomena.evidence: OLMo 2, Pythia (Biderman et al. 2023), BLOOM all published full curves. Variance from one batch to the next is small relative to trend.

phenomena.label: Grokking
phenomena.summary: Power et al. 2022 (arXiv:2201.02177): on small algorithmic tasks, models memorise the training set first, then much later — sometimes 100× more steps — generalise to held-out examples.
phenomena.evidence: Originally observed on modular arithmetic. Later replicated across many algorithmic tasks. Resists simple explanations; weight decay seems necessary.

phenomena.label: Phase transitions in capability
phenomena.summary: Specific abilities (induction, in-context learning, multi-digit arithmetic) appear suddenly at particular checkpoints rather than scaling smoothly with loss.
phenomena.evidence: Olsson et al. 2022 'In-Context Learning and Induction Heads' (Anthropic) shows induction-head circuits forming at a specific training step.

phenomena.label: Double descent
phenomena.summary: Belkin et al. 2019 (PNAS) and Nakkiran et al. 2020: test loss can rise, fall, rise, fall as model size or data size grows past the interpolation threshold.
phenomena.evidence: Robust across CNNs, ResNets, and Transformers. Tells us classical bias-variance intuition does not fully describe deep nets.

emergence._meta: The page's main intellectual conflict. Wei et al. 2022 vs Schaeffer, Miranda, Koyejo 2023.

wei_2022.title: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
wei_2022.claim: Some abilities (multi-step arithmetic, instruction-following on novel tasks, MMLU above ~50%) appear suddenly at specific scale thresholds rather than improving smoothly. Below the threshold, models perform near zero; above it, performance jumps to non-trivial levels.

schaeffer_2023.title: Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?
schaeffer_2023.claim: The 'emergence' is largely an artifact of measurement choices: discontinuous, all-or-nothing metrics like 'exact match accuracy' produce sharp jumps even when underlying token-level loss falls smoothly. Switch to a continuous metric (token-level cross-entropy, edit distance) and the curves smooth out.
schaeffer_2023.evidence: Reproduced 92% of claimed emergent jumps; showed the jumps disappear under continuous metrics. Ran controlled vision experiments where they could induce or remove emergence by changing the metric.

emergence.where_we_are: Most working researchers now treat both as partly right. The mirage critique is convincing for many specific 'emergence' claims, but Olsson et al.'s induction-head work shows real, sharp circuit-formation events in training. Practical takeaway: do not bet a product on a benchmark with a discrete metric; do read the underlying log-prob curve.

alignment._meta: Post-pretraining toolkit. Each method addresses a different problem; modern frontier models stack several.

methods.label: Full fine-tuning
methods.solves: Adapt to a domain (medical, legal, code)
methods.tradeoffs: Forgets pretraining knowledge; expensive; one fine-tune per task

methods.label: LoRA
methods.solves: Cheap, modular fine-tuning; many adapters per base model
methods.tradeoffs: Slightly less expressive than full fine-tuning; requires choosing rank

methods.label: QLoRA
methods.compute_relative: Fits a 65B fine-tune on one 48GB GPU
methods.solves: Run LoRA on consumer hardware via 4-bit quantization

methods.label: Instruction tuning (SFT)
methods.solves: Teach the model to follow instructions and respond like an assistant
methods.tradeoffs: Quality of demos sets the ceiling
methods.citation: Wei et al. 2021 FLAN, Sanh et al. 2021 T0

methods.label: RLHF (PPO-style)
methods.solves: Match nuanced human preferences (helpfulness, tone) where SFT can't
methods.tradeoffs: Reward hacking, sycophancy; needs separate reward model
methods.citation: Christiano et al. 2017 (arXiv:1706.03741), Ouyang et al. 2022 (arXiv:2203.02155) InstructGPT

methods.label: DPO
methods.solves: RLHF without the reward model, no PPO instability
methods.tradeoffs: Less of a 'true RL' training signal; some limitations on harder reward shapes

methods.label: RLAIF
methods.data_size: Synthetic preferences from a strong model
methods.solves: Scale preference data without paying humans

methods.label: Constitutional AI
methods.solves: Harmlessness without human labels for every harmful prompt
methods.tradeoffs: Constitution writing is itself a hard alignment problem

methods.label: Reasoning-RL (o1 / R1)
methods.compute_relative: 1–10% of pretraining for training; inference compute is the main cost
methods.solves: Long, deliberate chain-of-thought reasoning learned as a policy
methods.tradeoffs: Inference latency × N; not all domains have automatic verifiers

benchmarks._meta: Frontier model scores on the canonical capability benchmarks over time. Scores from official model cards, Stanford HAI AI Index 2025, Papers With Code, and the underlying benchmark leaderboards.

datasets.label: MMLU
datasets.notes: Massive multitask language understanding (Hendrycks et al. 2020)

datasets.label: HumanEval
datasets.notes: Function-level Python coding (Chen et al. 2021). Saturated because of contamination + ease.

datasets.label: ARC-AGI
datasets.notes: Abstraction & reasoning corpus (Chollet 2019); ARC-AGI-2 still <5% for frontier

datasets.label: FrontierMath
datasets.notes: Research-level math (Glazer et al. 2024). Frontier still <10%.

coda._meta: Two short threads worth flagging without a chart: mechanistic interpretability and the 'next-token prediction' debate.

interp.label: What's actually inside
interp.body: Mechanistic interpretability has gone from speculative to publishable since 2022. Anthropic's circuits work, sparse autoencoders, and Olsson et al.'s induction heads (2022) have identified concrete sub-circuits in real models. We can now name specific neurons that fire on specific concepts. We still cannot fully read a model's reasoning, but the 'pile of weights' framing is no longer accurate for small models.
papers: Olsson et al. 2022 — In-Context Learning and Induction Heads (Anthropic)

next_token.label: Is next-token prediction enough?
next_token.body: LeCun argues no — pure autoregressive prediction can't learn world models, planning, or physical reasoning, and we need different objectives (JEPA family). Sutskever and Karpathy argue yes — sufficiently scaled next-token prediction implicitly learns world models because predicting text well requires modelling what produced it. Both sides have produced strong empirical work; the debate is unresolved as of 2026.
papers: LeCun 2022 — A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence
Andreas 2024 — Language Models, World Models, and Human Model-Building

receipts.label: Schaeffer 2023 result
receipts.sub: of 'emergent' jumps disappear under continuous metrics
